mcqs.question: In Programmed I/O, the CPU continuously polls the I/O device to check if data is ready. What is the main disadvantage of this approach?

options.text: It requires additional hardware complexity
CPU is wasted in busy-wait loops, reducing overall system efficiency
Data transfer rate is too fast for the CPU to handle
It cannot handle multiple I/O devices simultaneously

mcqs.explanation: Programmed I/O wastes CPU cycles by repeatedly checking device status, making the CPU unavailable for other tasks during I/O operations.

mcqs.question: In interrupt-driven I/O, when does the CPU actually process the data transfer?

options.text: Immediately after the I/O device is connected
When the CPU finishes its current instruction cycle
Only when the operating system schedules it
After the device raises an interrupt signal to request service

mcqs.explanation: In interrupt-driven I/O, the device notifies the CPU via an interrupt only when it needs service, allowing the CPU to perform other tasks in the meantime.

mcqs.question: Which I/O method provides the highest data transfer rate with minimal CPU overhead?

options.text: Programmed I/O
Interrupt-driven I/O
DMA (Direct Memory Access)
Memory-mapped I/O

mcqs.explanation: DMA allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, eliminating CPU overhead for large data transfers.

mcqs.question: A DMA controller handles data transfer between an I/O device and memory. What is the primary role of the CPU in this process?

options.text: Directly controlling each data word transfer
Initializing the DMA controller with transfer parameters and handling interrupts upon completion
Converting data between different formats
Managing device driver installation

mcqs.explanation: The CPU programs the DMA controller with source/destination addresses and byte count, then performs other tasks while DMA handles the actual transfer.

mcqs.question: What happens when multiple I/O devices raise interrupts simultaneously in a system?

options.text: All interrupts are processed simultaneously
The interrupt with highest priority is serviced first based on priority encoder logic
The CPU processes them in the order they were raised
The operating system reboots to handle the situation

mcqs.explanation: Interrupt controllers use priority encoding to determine which device gets serviced first when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously.

mcqs.question: In DMA transfer between a disk and memory, what is the key difference between 'burst mode' and 'cycle stealing' mode?

options.text: Burst mode is slower than cycle stealing
Burst mode gives DMA exclusive bus control for entire transfer, while cycle stealing interleaves DMA transfers with CPU memory cycles
Cycle stealing requires more CPU intervention than burst mode
There is no difference; both terms mean the same thing

mcqs.explanation: Burst mode DMA blocks the CPU from bus access during transfer (high throughput, may cause CPU starvation), while cycle stealing alternates access (moderate throughput, minimal CPU impact).

mcqs.question: A data acquisition system samples a sensor at 100 kHz, storing each sample to memory. Which I/O technique is most appropriate and why?

options.text: Programmed I/O - simple to implement
Interrupt-driven I/O - sensor data arrives at predictable intervals
DMA - high data rate requires direct memory transfer without CPU overhead per sample
Memory-mapped I/O - provides fastest access

mcqs.explanation: At 100 kHz sampling rate, interrupt-driven I/O would generate too many interrupts (100,000/second), overwhelming the CPU; DMA handles this high-speed data stream efficiently.

mcqs.question: Consider a keyboard interface in a personal computer. Which combination of I/O techniques is typically used and why?

options.text: Programmed I/O for keyboard because data rate is very low
Interrupt-driven I/O for keyboard because key presses are asynchronous events that should not be polled
DMA for keyboard because character-by-character transfer is frequent
DMA for keyboard because it reduces power consumption

mcqs.explanation: Keyboard input is sporadic and event-driven; interrupt-driven I/O allows the CPU to respond immediately to key presses without wasting cycles polling for input that may not exist.
